stimulus: Within the canon of postcolonial Caribbean literature, Martinican philosopher Frantz Fanon’s 1952 book Black Skin, White Masks (Peau noire, masques blancs) occupies a singular position. A psychological study, a political polemic, and an unflinching look into the legacies of European empire, ______blank dissects the architecture of colonial identity with searing precision.
stem: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

answerOptions.content: France produced a wave of Caribbean intellectuals just before the publication of Fanon’s first major work, which
Fanon wrote a first major work that, published just before independence movements swept across French Africa,
Fanon’s first major work, published just before independence movements swept across French Africa,
published just before independence movements swept across French Africa, Fanon wrote a first major work that

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. Subject-modifier placement requires a modifier and its subject to be next to each other. The subject of the modifier "A psychological study...European empire" is Fanon’s book Black Skin, White Masks, so the subject "Fanon’s first major work" fits perfectly after this introductory modifying phrase.

Choice A is incorrect because the introductory modifier describes Fanon’s book, not France; this choice places "France" directly next to the modifier. Choice B is incorrect because the modifier describes Fanon’s book, not Fanon himself; this choice places "Fanon" directly next to the modifier. Choice D is incorrect because the modifier describes Fanon’s book; this choice places "Fanon" right after the modifier rather than the work itself.
